Importing and understanding the dataset :¶

In [ ]:
# pandas for analysis and reading the file :
import pandas as pd
# for visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
ola_data = pd.read_csv('Ola rides.csv')

  • Numerical features : date , time , distance , ride_charge , misc_charge , total_fare , duration
  • Categorical features : services , ride_status , source , destination , payment_method

(ride_id is neither categorical nor numerical as it just an id of the driver and it is unique for every ride)

In [ ]:
# the date and time feature stores string which can be an issue for x with date analysis
# so convert the date + time type (string) to datetime :
# make a copy first :
df_copy = ola_data.copy()

df_copy.date = pd.to_datetime(df_copy.date)
df_copy.time = pd.to_datetime(df_copy.time, format='%H:%M:%S.%f').dt.time
  • Normalizing the categorical columns : services , ride_status , payment_method , source , destination
In [ ]:
print(df_copy.services.unique())
print(df_copy.ride_status.unique())
print(df_copy.payment_method.unique())
# They have unique values so dont need any changes
# however source and destination contain a lot of labels so we normalize them
# for no category duplication :
df_copy['source'] = df_copy['source'].str.strip().str.lower()
df_copy['destination'] = df_copy['destination'].str.strip().str.lower()

['cab economy' 'auto' 'parcel' 'bike lite' 'bike']
['completed' 'cancelled']
['Amazon Pay' 'Paytm' nan 'QR scan' 'GPay']
In [ ]:
# check for null values in rides whose ride_status is comleted :
df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'completed'].isnull().sum()
# therefore no such null value exists in completed rides

  • The missing values (5036 for ride_charge, misc_charge, total_fare, and payment_method) have occured because 5036 rides were cancelled so logically their payments and other charges should not exist and be NaN

  • No such invalid data exists in the dataset , so we can separate the cancelled and completed rides for better understanding .
In [ ]:
completed_rides = df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'completed']
cancelled_rides = df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'cancelled']
In [ ]:
print(completed_rides.duplicated().sum())
print(cancelled_rides.duplicated().sum())
# no duplicated entry exists

0
0
  • It seems that total_fare = ride_charge + misc_charge

We shall check it before dropping them

In [ ]:
completed_rides['check'] = (completed_rides['ride_charge'] + completed_rides['misc_charge'])

(completed_rides.check == completed_rides.total_fare).value_counts()

  completed_rides['check'] = (completed_rides['ride_charge'] + completed_rides['misc_charge'])

count
True 39837
False 5127

In [ ]:
  # Mismatch exists , so we shall recalculate the total_fare column values
  # in df_copy so no mismatch exist .
df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'completed']['total_fare'] = df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'completed']['ride_charge'] + df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'completed']['misc_charge']

/tmp/ipython-input-3077412111.py:3: SettingWithCopyWarning: 
A value is trying to be set on a copy of a slice from a DataFrame.
Try using .loc[row_indexer,col_indexer] = value instead

See the caveats in the documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/user_guide/indexing.html#returning-a-view-versus-a-copy
  df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'completed']['total_fare'] = df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'completed']['ride_charge'] + df_copy[df_copy.ride_status == 'completed']['misc_charge']
  • Creating columns that stores the day and month of the ride (allows for better analysis):
In [ ]:
df_copy['day'] = df_copy.date.dt.day_name()
df_copy['month'] = df_copy.date.dt.month_name()
In [ ]:
# rearraging the columns :
new_order = ['services', 'date','day', 'month', 'time', 'ride_status',
             'source', 'destination','duration',
             'ride_id', 'distance', 'ride_charge', 'misc_charge',
            'total_fare', 'payment_method']

df_copy = df_copy[new_order]

SUMMARY :

  • The dataset contained 5036 null values only in the rows where the ride_status was labelled 'cancelled' . This means the null values are rightfully placed .

  • The categorical columns were normalized .

  • Converted Date and time to proper formats

  • Corrected the total_fare values (total_fare = ride_charge + misc_charge)

  • Introduced two new features : day (that stores the day of the ride ) and month (stores the month of the ride)

The cleaned dataset has 15 columns and 50000 rows with info like fare charges , distance covered, duration spent (likely in minutes) and so on .

Categorical features analysis :¶

In [ ]:
# checking the ride_status :
print(df_copy.ride_status.value_counts(normalize = True) * 100)

sns.countplot(data= df_copy , x = 'ride_status')
plt.title('Ride Status')
plt.show()

ride_status
completed    89.928
cancelled    10.072
Name: proportion, dtype: float64

Interpretation :

Majority of the rides taken by Ola were completed . A small percentage (about 10%) were not completed.

In [ ]:
# setting up figure size :
fig , axes = plt.subplots(nrows = 2 , ncols = 2 , figsize = (18,12))
fig.suptitle('Univariate Analysis of categorical features' , fontsize = 16)

# plotting for each categorical column :
sns.countplot(ax = axes[0,0] , x = 'services' , data = df_copy ).set_title('Services Distribution')
sns.countplot(ax = axes[0,1] , x = 'payment_method' , data = df_copy ).set_title('Payment Method Distribution')
sns.countplot(ax = axes[1,0] , x = 'month' , data = df_copy , order = ['June' , 'July' , 'August'] ).set_title('Count of rides in every month')
sns.countplot(ax = axes[1,1] , x = 'day' , data = df_copy ,
                                                            order=['Monday','Tuesday','Wednesday','Thursday',
                                                                  'Friday','Saturday','Sunday'
                                                                  ]).set_title('Count of rides in every day of week')

plt.tight_layout(rect = [0 , 0 , 1 , 0.96])
plt.show()

Interpretation pf categorical features :

  • Service : Bike Rides the most commonly opted rides followed by auto and cabs . Probably because bikes are faster in arrival and dropping you off . Although the age feature is not given , it can be said that most of the passengers opting for bike rides must be working proffesionals who need to reach their workplace on time .

  • Payment Method : payment method have almost equal share i.e mobile payments only . This shows that all the passengers carried smartphones .

  • Month : July recorded the majority number of rides . This must be as July is the peak of monsoon period and people tend to take cabs or different modes of transport other than public transport to avoid the discomfort they might experience there. Also Ola provides the service of dropping the consumer to their doorstep which is not provided by public transport.

  • Day : The rides taken on weekends are slightly lesser than the weekdays . (logically because people tend to rest on weekends)

In [ ]:
# taking out the top 10 locations of source and destination:
top_10_sources = df_copy['source'].value_counts().index[:10]
top_10_destinations = df_copy['destination'].value_counts().index[:10]

# plotting for source and destinations:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=2, ncols=1, figsize=(18, 16))
fig.suptitle('Top 10 Sources and Destinations' , fontsize = 16)

sns.countplot(ax=axes[0], x='source', data=df_copy, order=top_10_sources)
axes[0].set_title('Top 10 Sources from where customer is picked')
axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45) # Removed 'ha' argument

sns.countplot(ax=axes[1], x='destination', data=df_copy, order=top_10_destinations)
axes[1].set_title('Top 10 Destinations where customer is dropped')
axes[1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45) # Removed 'ha' argument

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96]) # Adjust layout to prevent overlap
plt.show()

Interpretation :

  • Source : kothanur landing was the most visited site for picking up passengers .
  • Destination : Gottigere landing was the most dropped off location .

These both are the most visited due to high connectivity to the city and their distance from the metro , thus allowing commuters to opt for rides the most from these locations .

# Analyzing numerical features: duration, distance, total_fare

fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=3, ncols=1, figsize=(18, 15))
fig.suptitle('Distribution of Numerical Features', fontsize=16)

sns.histplot(ax=axes[0], data=df_copy, x='duration', bins = 30 , kde=True)
axes[0].set_title('Duration Distribution')

sns.histplot(ax=axes[1], data=df_copy, x='distance', bins = 30 , kde=True)
axes[1].set_title('Distance Distribution')

sns.histplot(ax=axes[2], data=df_copy, x='total_fare', bins = 30 , kde=True)
axes[2].set_title('Total Fare Distribution')

plt.show()

Interpretation :

  • Mean : distance -> 25.5 , duration -> 64.3 , total fare -> 547.4

  • The mean of all these features tend to be very close to their corresponding median value meaning that these features are symmetric (since mean is apporixmately equal to the median [50%]) . So there is no skew or exception cases .

  • This means that there were equally short and long rides

  • There were also costly rides but eqaully cheap rides to balance out .

# we shall find the cost per kilometer of every service to measure which is expensive :
# CPK = total_fare / distance

df_copy['CPK'] = df_copy['total_fare'] / df_copy['distance']
df_copy.groupby('services').CPK.min()

In [ ]:
# what is the duration covered by all services ?
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
for service_type in df_copy['services'].unique():
    sns.kdeplot(df_copy[df_copy['services'] == service_type]['duration'].dropna(), label=service_type)

plt.title('KDE Plot of Duration by Service Type')
plt.xlabel('Duration')
plt.ylabel('Density')
plt.legend(title='Service Type')
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.show()

Interpretations :

  • The overlapping areas between the KDE curves indicate that a ride of a particular duration (e.g., a 30-minute ride or a 60-minute ride) could be completed by any of the service types (auto, bike, cab economy, etc.). This suggests that customers use different services for similar duration journeys.

In [ ]:
# which route earns the highest total fare and what is the total distance for those routes?
route_earnings = df_copy.groupby(['source', 'destination']).agg(
    total_fare=('total_fare', 'sum'),
    total_distance=('distance', 'sum')
).sort_values(by='total_fare', ascending=False)[:10]
# display(route_earnings)

plt.scatter(x = route_earnings.total_fare , y = route_earnings.total_distance)
for i in range(len(route_earnings)):
  plt.text(x = route_earnings.total_fare[i] + 0.2 , y = route_earnings.total_distance[i],
           s = f"{route_earnings.total_fare[i]} , {route_earnings.total_distance[i]}")
plt.title('Total Fare vs Total Distance')

plt.xlabel('Total Fare')
plt.ylabel('Total Distance')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

/tmp/ipython-input-3028544705.py:10: FutureWarning: Series.__getitem__ treating keys as positions is deprecated. In a future version, integer keys will always be treated as labels (consistent with DataFrame behavior). To access a value by position, use `ser.iloc[pos]`
  plt.text(x = route_earnings.total_fare[i] + 0.2 , y = route_earnings.total_distance[i],
/tmp/ipython-input-3028544705.py:11: FutureWarning: Series.__getitem__ treating keys as positions is deprecated. In a future version, integer keys will always be treated as labels (consistent with DataFrame behavior). To access a value by position, use `ser.iloc[pos]`
  s = f"{route_earnings.total_fare[i]} , {route_earnings.total_distance[i]}")

Interpretation :

  • From rmv 2nd stage 1st stage to jakkasandra viewpoint , the price was very high for the small distance as relative to the other rates

  • We can say that this above pricing is because the source ( rmv 2nd stage 1st stage) is a premium society where above middle class people live. So they can afford this amount as compared to an average earner , which counts for the reason behind the high price for the small distance .

  • This is the same reason behind the high price for the route thanisandra summit to jakkasandra 4th stage as the source is also a high residential area .

In [ ]:
# how much percent of services were cancelled ?
print('percentage of the cancelled rides for every service')
print(cancelled_rides.services.value_counts(normalize = True) * 100)
print('\nrates of completed rides to the cancelled rides :')
print(
((cancelled_rides.services.value_counts() /df_copy.services.value_counts()) * 100).sort_values(ascending = False))

percentage of the cancelled rides for every service
services
bike           30.996823
auto           24.086577
cab economy    20.929309
parcel         14.138205
bike lite       9.849087
Name: proportion, dtype: float64

rates of completed rides to the cancelled rides :
services
cab economy    10.331308
bike           10.318614
bike lite      10.155610
auto            9.840188
parcel          9.545515
Name: count, dtype: float64

Interpretation :

  • Although bike showed the most cancellation share , the cab economy actually had a most share of cancellations among its own ride bookings , followed by bike cancellations .

  • This shows that cab and bike need to improve their services to decrease their cancellation rate

  • The reason why bike cancellations were that largest was because the services of bike rides was mostly opted , thus increasing its relative cancellation share . But the ratio between cancellation and total ride bookings gives a clear view that cab economy was facing most of the cancellations.

Conclusion :¶

The dataset reveals a healthy operational ecosystem with a 90% ride completion rate. While the service is heavily driven by short-to-mid-distance commuting (average ~25km), there are significant opportunities to optimize the "Cab Economy" segment and address seasonal demand spikes.

1. Operational Performance & Reliability¶

  • Completion vs. Cancellation: Approximately 10.07% of rides are cancelled.
  • The "Cab" Problem: Although "Bikes" have the highest total number of cancellations (due to their high volume), the Cab Economy segment actually suffers from the highest cancellation rate relative to its own bookings. This suggests a supply-side issue or driver reluctance in the economy car segment.
  • Data Integrity: The dataset is remarkably clean after initial processing, with "null" values correctly aligning only with cancelled trips where no payment was processed.

2. User Behavior & Preferences¶

  • Dominant Mode: Bike Rides are the most popular choice, followed by Autos. This indicates a user base that prioritizes speed and cost-effectiveness for city transit.
  • Digital-First Audience: There is a near-perfect split between various mobile payment methods (GPay, Paytm, Amazon Pay). The absence of "Cash" as a dominant outlier (and the presence of QR scans) suggests a tech-savvy user demographic.
  • Peak Demand: July emerged as the busiest month. This is likely correlated with the monsoon season in India, where users shift from walking or public transport to doorstep services like Ola to avoid rain.

3. Pricing & Financial Insights¶

  • Symmetric Pricing: The average fare is ₹547, with distance and duration showing a symmetric distribution (mean median). This means there are no extreme skews—the system handles short "quick" trips and long "commutes" equally well.

  • Service Efficiency:

  • Cab Economy has the highest Cost per Kilometer (CPK) at ~1.34.

  • Auto is the most economical motorized four/three-wheel option at ~1.19 CPK.

  • Premium Routing: Specific routes (e.g., RMV 2nd Stage to Jakkasandra) show significantly higher pricing for shorter distances. This indicates the algorithm successfully implements location-based surge pricing in high-income or high-demand residential zones.

4. Geographic Hotspots¶

  • Primary Hubs: Kothanur Landing is the most frequent pickup point, while Gottigere Landing is the top drop-off destination. These locations likely act as major transit feeders or residential hubs.

Final Recommendations¶

  1. Reduce Cab Cancellations: Investigate why "Cab Economy" drivers cancel more frequently. Potential solutions include better driver incentives for short-trip economy rides or stricter "cancellation-cap" policies.
  2. Monsoon Scaling: Since July shows a massive spike in demand, Ola should increase "driver-on-road" counts and offer rain-related protective gear for Bike/Auto partners to maintain supply during peak monsoon weeks.
  3. Targeted Marketing: High-value routes (like RMV 2nd Stage) are clearly profitable. Offering loyalty "Passes" for these specific high-income corridors could lock in high-LTV (Lifetime Value) customers.

Verdict: The analysis is complete and logically sound. The notebook successfully transformed raw ride data into actionable business intelligence regarding service health and pricing strategy.
